title: Rotational Mechanics
learningObjectives: Define torque and calculate it using τ = Fr sinθ
Calculate moment of inertia for simple shapes
Apply the rotational analogue of Newton's second law: τ = Iα
State and apply conservation of angular momentum
introText: Rotational mechanics extends the concepts of linear mechanics to rotating objects. Every linear quantity has a rotational analogue: force → torque, mass → moment of inertia, velocity → angular velocity.

steps.heading: Torque
steps.body:
Torque (τ) is the rotational equivalent of force. It measures the tendency of a force to cause rotation:

τ = Fr sinθ

where F is the applied force, r is the perpendicular distance from the pivot (moment arm), and θ is the angle between F and r.

Torque is measured in Newton-metres (N·m).
steps.deepDive: For maximum torque, apply the force perpendicular to the moment arm (θ = 90°, sinθ = 1). This is why door handles are placed far from the hinge.

steps.heading: Moment of Inertia
steps.body:
Moment of inertia (I) is the rotational analogue of mass — it measures resistance to angular acceleration.

For a point mass: I = mr²
Solid disk: I = ½MR²
Hollow cylinder: I = MR²
Solid sphere: I = ²⁄₅MR²

Rotational Newton's second law:
τ = Iα

where α is angular acceleration (rad/s²).
steps.deepDive: The distribution of mass matters for I. Two objects of the same total mass can have very different moments of inertia depending on how the mass is distributed.

steps.heading: Rotational KE and Angular Momentum
steps.body:
Rotational kinetic energy:
KE_rot = ½Iω²

Angular momentum:
L = Iω

Conservation of angular momentum: If no external torque acts, L is constant.
I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂

Example: a spinning ice skater pulls their arms in, reducing I, which increases ω to conserve L.
steps.deepDive: Rolling objects have both translational and rotational KE. Total KE = ½mv² + ½Iω². For a rolling ball, this means it rolls more slowly down a ramp than a sliding block.

steps.questionText: A torque of 12 N·m acts on a disk with moment of inertia 4 kg·m². What is the angular acceleration?

questions.questionText: A skater spins with I = 2 kg·m² at ω = 3 rad/s. She pulls her arms in to I = 0.5 kg·m². What is her new angular velocity?
questions.explanation: Conservation: I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂. 2 × 3 = 0.5 × ω₂. ω₂ = 6/0.5 = 12 rad/s.
